title: RAID Capacity & Failure Impact Guide
summary: Understand how RAID level, drive count, hot spares, overhead, and imported storage demand affect usable capacity and retention margin.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates usable RAID capacity, retention support, and failure-state exposure from the selected storage layout.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it after sizing storage demand so the design can be checked against redundancy, overhead, spare strategy, and usable capacity.
bullets: Early video-storage design planning.
Expansion review or recorder replacement.
Existing-system cleanup, validation, or troubleshooting.
Explaining storage, retention, and quality tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on this tool and explain what each value represents in practical video-storage planning terms.

items.label: RAID level
items.description: The redundancy layout used for the storage set.
items.whyItMatters: RAID level changes usable capacity, fault tolerance, and failure behavior.
items.commonMistake: Choosing RAID only by capacity without considering rebuild exposure or failure tolerance.

items.label: Drive count
items.description: The number of drives in the RAID group or storage set.
items.whyItMatters: Drive count affects raw capacity, usable capacity, and sometimes performance/rebuild exposure.
items.commonMistake: Adding drives without reviewing whether the RAID level is still appropriate.

items.label: Drive size (TB)
items.description: The capacity of each drive in the set.
items.whyItMatters: Drive size affects total usable storage and potential rebuild duration.
items.commonMistake: Using larger disks only for capacity while ignoring recovery time and degraded-state exposure.

items.label: Hot spares
items.description: The number of drives reserved as automatic replacements instead of usable data drives.
items.whyItMatters: Hot spares reduce usable capacity but can reduce time before rebuild begins.
items.commonMistake: Counting hot spares as if they were usable storage.

items.label: Platform overhead (%)
items.description: Capacity reserved or lost to filesystem behavior, VMS reserve, snapshots, metadata, and operational margin.
items.whyItMatters: Platform overhead reduces the storage available for footage.
items.commonMistake: Reporting raw RAID capacity instead of usable capacity after platform overhead.

links.label: Use the same reserve model in storage sizing
links.note: Keep storage and RAID reserve assumptions aligned.

items.label: Target retention (days)
items.description: The retention goal the RAID capacity should support.
items.whyItMatters: Target retention lets usable capacity be compared against the design promise.
items.commonMistake: Assuming a storage array is large enough without comparing it to the retention target.

items.label: Imported required storage (GB)
items.description: The required capacity carried from an upstream storage or retention calculation.
items.whyItMatters: Imported demand gives the RAID tool a real capacity target instead of only showing raw usable capacity.
items.commonMistake: Checking RAID capacity without comparing it to the actual video-storage requirement.

links.label: Size required storage first
links.note: Use this when no upstream required-storage value is available.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means usable capacity and retention margin appear acceptable. Watch means overhead, spare strategy, or failure exposure should be reviewed. Risk means the RAID layout may not support the required storage or retention promise reliably.
bullets: Healthy: the design appears manageable based on the entered assumptions.
Watch: the design may still work, but the assumptions deserve review.
Risk: add capacity, reduce demand, adjust policy, or validate the design more carefully before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most video-storage planning problems come from treating bitrate, storage, retention, RAID, and failure behavior as separate decisions even though they shape the same final retention promise.
bullets: Sizing storage from one average bitrate without checking peak, motion, codec, and retention behavior.
Treating nominal capacity as usable capacity after RAID, filesystem, spare, and operational reserves.
Ignoring growth, camera additions, quality changes, and retention policy changes after day one.
Failing to document assumptions before the recorder, storage tier, or archive policy is selected.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, VMS platform limits, camera-specific behavior, storage vendor guidance, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Camera bitrate can vary significantly with scene motion, lighting, compression settings, and codec behavior.
Usable storage should account for redundancy, reserved space, filesystem overhead, and operational margin.
Retention targets should be validated against stakeholder expectations, evidence needs, and system operation.
A design with documented headroom is usually easier to support, expand, and troubleshoot.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal design review, manufacturer validation, field testing, or project-specific responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual VMS, recorder, camera model, codec profile, storage platform, and network path being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations for retention, archive access, failure behavior, and acceptable image quality.
Document assumptions before implementation so future changes can be evaluated against the original design intent.
